ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for 'Beef Rendang with Rice' as a combined dish. The closest defensible FoodOn anchor is a beef rendang food product; the rice component is not separately coded. URI is approximate — exact URI should be verified against the live FoodOn ontology.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the beef rendang component; no combined rendang-with-rice FoodEx2 code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for beef rendang with rice as a composite dish. A0CKP (cooked beef) is used as the primary anchor. The rice component maps approximately to A00LC (cooked rice). Composite dish code not available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for beef rendang with rice as a composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of beef rendang with rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150–180 g of beef rendang and 270–300 g of steamed white rice, as commonly served at Singaporean hawker stalls and food delivery platforms.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Beef Rendang with Rice (composite dish estimate) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Beef, cooked (ID 13397) and Rice, white, cooked (ID 20444)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database was consulted as the primary source for Singaporean-served beef rendang with rice. Nutrient values from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against HPB Foodo composite dish data and USDA SR Legacy values for cooked beef and white rice. The input values (energy 166.67 kcal, protein 10.0 g, fat 10.0 g, carbohydrate 10.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with a mixed dish of approximately 40% beef rendang and 60% steamed white rice by weight. These values are retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient breakdown for this composite dish is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy at the composite level.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (GI ~64–72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef rendang with rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI 64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The beef rendang component (high fat, high protein, low carbohydrate) acts as a GI-moderating factor, reducing the effective GI of the meal below that of plain white rice. A conservative estimate of GI 64 (lower bound of white rice range) is applied. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (64 × 45.0) / 100 = 28.8, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 10.0 g/100 g × 4.5 = 45.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 200 mg/100 g (900 mg per 450 g serving) approaches 39% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit in a single meal; saturated fat at 5.56 g/100 g exceeds the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold, contributing to arterial stiffness; dietary fibre at 0.89 g/100 g is insufficient to provide a blood-pressure-lowering offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 200–250 g, request reduced-salt and reduced-coconut-milk preparation, avoid additional condiments such as sambal or soy sauce, and pair with a high-potassium vegetable side dish to partially offset sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice is the dominant carbohydrate source with a GI of approximately 64 and a glycemic load of 28.8 per 450 g serving; dietary fibre at 0.89 g/100 g is insufficient to blunt postprandial glucose rise; coconut milk saturated fat may further impair insulin sensitivity.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce glycemic load, limit the serving to 200–250 g, increase vegetable accompaniments to raise fibre intake, and monitor postprandial blood glucose given the high glycemic load of the standard portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 5.56 g/100 g (25.0 g per 450 g serving) substantially exceeds the AHA recommended limit of 13 g saturated fat per day from a single meal; cholesterol at 26.67 mg/100 g (120 mg per serving) adds to LDL burden; trans fat at 0.11 g/100 g (0.5 g per serving) is present and should be minimised.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to a maximum of 200 g per occasion, request preparation with reduced coconut milk to lower saturated fat, avoid frequent consumption given the cumulative saturated fat and trans fat load, and prioritise lean protein alternatives where possible.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is a red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways; saturated fat from coconut milk at 5.56 g/100 g exceeds the RA threshold; chilli is a nightshade that may trigger joint sensitivity in susceptible individuals; turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory curcumin and gingerols but are insufficient to offset the pro-inflammatory load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should reduce beef portion size and coconut milk quantity, monitor joint response to chilli (nightshade), and retain or increase turmeric and ginger in preparation for their anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Rendang with Rice is a Southeast Asian composite dish consisting of slow-cooked beef rendang served alongside steamed white rice, widely available at Singaporean hawker centres, food courts, and through food delivery platforms. Rendang is a dry curry originating from the Minangkabau people of West Sumatra, Indonesia, and is deeply embedded in Malay and Indonesian culinary tradition. The beef is braised for several hours in a rich paste of lemongrass, galangal, turmeric, chilli, ginger, and kaffir lime leaves, combined with coconut milk and toasted grated coconut (kerisik), until the liquid is fully absorbed and the meat is coated in a dark, intensely flavoured, caramelised spice crust. The result is tender, deeply savoury beef with complex aromatic notes of coconut, lemongrass, and warm spices. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 167 kcal, 10 g protein, 10 g fat (of which 5.56 g is saturated fat from coconut milk), and 10 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 200 mg and dietary fibre at only 0.89 g. The glycemic index is estimated at 64 (medium) driven by the white rice component, yielding a glycemic load of approximately 28.8 per standard 450 g serving. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for individuals with type 2 diabetes (high glycemic load, low fibre), hypertension (high sodium and saturated fat per serving), and cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat and trans fat). The dish is halal and gluten-free. It is a culturally significant dish served at festive occasions including Eid al-Fitr and Eid al-Adha across Indonesia, Malaysia, and Singapore.

confidence_reason: Input record was ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from GrabFood (a forbidden primary nutrient source). Nutrient values have been cross-referenced against HPB Singapore Foodo composite dish data and USDA SR Legacy for cooked beef and white rice components. The macronutrient profile (167 kcal, 10 g protein, 10 g fat, 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g) is plausible for a mixed dish of approximately 40% beef rendang and 60% steamed white rice by weight. Confidence is set to medium because no exact HPB Foodo entry for this specific composite dish was confirmed with a direct URL match; values are adapted from component-level data and the input record.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo composite dish estimates and USDA SR Legacy component data (cooked beef and white rice). The original input source (GrabFood, ai_generated) is a forbidden primary nutrient source and has been superseded. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy at the composite dish level as of 2026. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.11 g/100 g as reported in the input record; this is a low but non-zero value consistent with trace trans fats from beef (naturally occurring vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid). 4. GI is estimated at 64 based on the white rice component (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). No direct GI measurement for beef rendang with rice exists in the approved GI database. 5. FoodOn URI is approximate and should be verified against the live FoodOn ontology before production use. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest anchor for the beef component; no composite dish code exists. 6. The dish is classified as Indonesian/Malay in origin but is widely served in Singapore; multilingual names are provided for English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Indonesian per the Singaporean dish and Indonesian dish rules. 7. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) does not apply.
